schools.summary: Gill sifts the datings: Josephus's Eli is too late for Boaz, and the Jewish chronology placing the famine under Eglon and Ehud comes nearer the truth.
Henry reads the famine as threatened judgment and censures Elimelech's removal as a discontented, distrustful spirit, while Providence keeps an eye on Bethlehem and the Messiah.
Clarke and Wesley leave the judge uncertain (Ussher chose Shamgar) but insist the events are early, since Boaz was born of Rahab and Christ descends from two Gentile mothers.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch place the story comparatively early in the judges period and explain the flight by Moab's exceptional fertility, though fleeing Jehovah's land was doubtful obedience.

debates.question: Under which judge did the famine and Elimelech's emigration fall?

sides.position: early, in the days of Eglon and Ehud (the Jewish chronology, Lightfoot)
in the days of Gideon, when the Midianite ravages caused famine
in the days of Shamgar (Ussher and most chronologers)

schools.summary: Gill hears in Elimelech, 'my God is King,' a confession of the theocracy of the judges, and thinks him a great man, kin to Salmon prince of Judah.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott parse the genuine Hebrew names (gracious, weakly, pining) and derive Ephrathite from Ephrath, Bethlehem's old name.
Clarke and Wesley gloss the names and see in the sickly sons 'the products of our pleasant things, weak and infirm, fading and dying.'
Henry reads Mahlon and Chilion, sickness and consumption, as a memento of the frailty of all earthly comforts.
Gill argues from the text, with Alshech against Josephus, that Elimelech died soon after arriving and the sons married only after his death.
Clarke agrees the death came probably a short time after the arrival in Moab.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the narrative pattern: the woman is progressively 'left' — first of her husband, then of her sons.
Henry: we cannot outrun death nor prosper out of the way of duty; death in a family often makes breach upon breach.

debates.question: When did Elimelech die — after ten years in Moab, or soon after arriving?

sides.position: after he had dwelt in the land ten years and married off his sons
quickly after he came into Moab; the ten years measure the sons' marriages

schools.summary: Clarke endorses the Targum's censure of the strange wives; Wesley allows that they were either proselytes or sinners punished with short life and want of issue.
Ellicott sees the father's fault carried on by the sons, though Keil and Delitzsch note marriage with Moabites was not forbidden in the law as with Canaanites.
Gill reports Aben Ezra's proselyte theory, thinks it true of Ruth at least afterwards, and rests in Providence ordering that Messiah might spring from one of them.
Henry: all agree this was ill done — the women were not proselytes, for Orpah returned to her gods.

debates.question: Were the marriages to Moabite women unlawful?

sides.position: a transgression — 'they transgressed the decree of the word of the Lord' (the Targum)
not forbidden in the law like Canaanite marriages, and the wives perhaps proselyted first

schools.summary: Clarke thinks the Targum's judgment — days cut off for the strange marriages — 'more than conjecture'; Wesley allows it as one reading.
Ellicott notes only that they died as quite young men: 'it is not for us to say' whether the deaths were punishment.
Gill records the Targum's censure without adopting it, noting the deaths after about ten years' dwelling.
Henry: loss of children and widowhood came in their perfection; death makes breach upon breach, that we may secure comforts death cannot take.

debates.question: Were the early deaths of Mahlon and Chilion a divine punishment for their Moabite marriages?

sides.position: very likely more than conjecture — their days were cut off in judgment
it is not for us to say; the text records the deaths without assigning the cause

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jehovah 'visited' His people by turning His favour to give bread; strictly, only Naomi can be said to 'return.' Ellicott thinks her conscience smote her for distrusting Israel's God.
Henry: plenty is God's gift; Naomi obeys the voice of the rod, and earth is embittered to us that heaven may be endeared.
Clarke relays the Targum (news by an angel, plenty for Ibzan's righteousness and Boaz's prayers); Wesley: she stayed no longer than necessity forced her.
Gill: she longed for the land where she could better serve the Lord, the daughters-in-law at first meaning only to accompany her part of the way.

outliers: Adam Clarke passes on the conjecture that Mahlon and Chilion are the Joash and Saraph of 1 Chronicles 4:22 who 'married in Moab.'

schools.summary: Gill: the family may have dwelt in Moab's fields rather than cities, whether by exclusion or to avoid idolatry; the daughters-in-law meant at first only to escort Naomi.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'return' strictly applies only to Naomi; Ellicott: both women clearly purposed to emigrate, since the parting could hardly but be final.
Henry: the shared road proves they had dwelt together in unity — Naomi an example to all mothers-in-law, love unhindered by difference of religion.

debates.question: Did Orpah and Ruth set out intending to emigrate to Judah, or only to escort Naomi part of the way?

sides.position: at first only to accompany her some part of the way
both clearly purposing to go with Naomi to the land of Israel

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: 'her mother's house' because maternal love knows best how to comfort; Ellicott: Naomi's love is all unselfish, refusing to sacrifice the young widows to her own solace.
Gill places the speech at the border of Moab and Israel, the natural point of parting.
Wesley: daughters dwelt in their mothers' apartments; 'the dead' are the sons, their husbands, while they lived.
Henry: a model parting — with commendation and with prayer, invoking Jehovah as the only fountain of good upon those who had dealt kindly.
Ellicott: a twofold blessing solemnized by the twofold name Jehovah; Keil and Delitzsch: the wish is simply that both be happily married again.
Gill rules the ancient supplements (a perfect reward, an husband) needless — the rest-wish itself is the sum of the blessing.
Henry: marriage is a state of rest such as this world affords, and God's gift — yet let God be the rest of the soul, no perfect rest on this side heaven.

themes: rest in the house of a husband
God the rest of the soul

schools.summary: Gill: the words come only when the women had eased themselves in cries and tears and recovered their speech.
Keil and Delitzsch: the opening particle strengthens the declaration into an emphatic 'surely we will return with thee.'
Henry: a rare affection to a mother-in-law — yet Orpah's identical protestation did not hold, for strong passions without a settled judgment produce weak resolutions.
Clarke and Wesley explain the levirate allusion — the ancient custom and express law of God, which Naomi had doubtless taught them.
Keil and Delitzsch state the legal point: no unborn brothers-in-law exist on whom the levirate obligation could rest; Ellicott hears Naomi painting the loneliness of her own lot.
Gill: the dissuasion is a deliberate trial, testing whether the women's resolve sprang from religion or mere natural affection.
Gill: the repeated dismissal is a second deliberate trial of whether real love to the God of Israel lay under their affection.
Keil and Delitzsch: Naomi escalates from no levirate hope to no prospect at all of husband or children.
Henry: she would have no converts who came upon her account — they must count the cost, for what is soon ripe is soon rotten.
Ellicott, with LXX, Peshito, and Targum, renders 'it is far more bitter for me than for you' — Naomi comparing her irreparable loss with theirs.
Gill: they could not be expected to tarry for sons unborn, as even Tamar's waiting for Shelah was for a child already living.
Wesley: her grief is for them; under the rod we are to hear its voice as if it spoke only to us, and converts must count the terms lest they look back from the plow.
Henry: she takes the affliction to herself — the sinner grieving most that others suffer by her — the mark of a gracious, generous spirit.

debates.question: How should 'it grieveth me much for your sakes' be rendered?

sides.position: 'it is far more bitter for me than for you' — with the Septuagint, Peshito Syriac, and Targum
grief for their sakes — the received rendering, following the Vulgate

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Ruth 'clung' with the cleaving-verb of Genesis 2:24, forsaking father and mother; Ellicott is sympathetic to Orpah, whom the comforts of home detain.
Clarke notes the LXX addition 'and returned to her own people'; Wesley: many love Christ, yet leave him, because they love other things better.
Henry: one dissuasion was to Orpah a savour of death unto death, to Ruth of life unto life — the parting kiss against the cleaving.
Gill: the renewed weeping shows both could hardly bear the parting — yet only one stayed.
Clarke: both women were probably idolaters, Ruth's conversion commencing now; Wesley: Naomi speaks to try Ruth's sincerity and to require the true religion of her.
Ellicott: Naomi views the Moabite idols as realities confined to Moab, her religion not yet enlightened beyond the God of Israel.
Gill: the speech is a deliberate proof of whether love to the God of Israel, not mere affection, carried Ruth forward.
Henry: forsaking the communion of saints ends in embracing the idols of Moab; Orpah's revolt makes the sincere manifest.

debates.question: Why does Naomi speak of Orpah returning 'unto her gods' without protest?

sides.position: she half-believes the idols real, her theology bounded by Moab's borders
a deliberate test of Ruth's sincerity, intimating she must embrace the true religion

debates.question: Had Orpah and Ruth been proselytes in Moab?

sides.position: probably both idolaters; the proselyte theory is unfounded conjecture
proselyted before or at marriage (Aben Ezra's view, reported earlier)

schools.summary: Clarke: a more perfect surrender was never made — country, fare, worship, and grave all yielded, evidently without any secular motive.
Henry: nothing finer or braver was ever said — the grace of God inclining the soul to the resolute choice of the better part; Ruth is the pattern of a resolute convert.
Ellicott: Ruth's nobleness is proof against all, her intensity the greater now that she pleads alone.
Gill: 'entreat me not' is literally 'be not against me' — no argument must stand in the way of her resolution.

themes: thy God shall be my God

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch identify the standard oath formula — death alone shall divide us; Ellicott: she swears by the name of the God of Israel, and Naomi yields.
Gill: one grave desired in hope of rising together at the resurrection of the just, the ancestral sepulchres renounced.
Wesley: joined souls desiring to mingle dust; Clarke: she proved true to her engagement, nursing Naomi's grandson in the house of Boaz.
Henry: death parts whom nothing else will — but our adherence to Christ must be closer still, whom death itself cannot separate.
Gill: Ruth's immovable steadfastness was the very thing Naomi's dissuasions were designed to prove.
Keil and Delitzsch: the verb means 'to stiffen one's self firmly upon a thing' — so Naomi gave up persuading her.
Wesley: the half-resolved are a door ajar inviting a thief; resolution bolts the door and the devil flees.
Henry: Naomi was not silenced but satisfied — resolution puts temptation to silence.
Ellicott measures the fifty-mile journey over Arnon and Jordan; Keil and Delitzsch: the town's women cry 'Is this Naomi?' amazed at her mournful condition, not her survival.
Gill: no beasts, no servants — the mean circumstances of the two who kept company the whole way.
Clarke: the stir proves Elimelech's family was of high consideration; Wesley: how marvellously is her condition changed.
Henry: 'Is this Naomi?' may be contempt or compassion — afflictions make great and surprising changes in a little time.
Ellicott: a deliberate Hebrew name-play — my-pleasantness against Mara the bitter (as at Marah's spring) — and Shaddai the All-Sufficient who gives and takes back.
Gill corrects Josephus: the names mean sweet and bitter, not prosperity and grief, though her states answer to both.
Clarke: Shaddai the self-sufficient has taken away the props and supports of her life; Wesley glosses the two names simply.
Henry: no murmuring but composure — he afflicts as a God in covenant, and he that empties us of the creature knows how to fill us with himself.
Gill: 'full' of husband and children, or children and riches; the rabbinic charge of covetous flight is made without any just cause.
Clarke: Elimelech took his property, on which Naomi subsisted till all was spent; Wesley: the LORD has borne witness in judgment and given sentence against her.
Henry: every rod has a voice, the voice of a witness — yet there is a spiritual fulness of which we can never be emptied.

debates.question: Was the family's 'going out full' a blameworthy, even covetous, flight?

sides.position: yes — they left to avoid relieving the poor, and were punished for it
the charge is without any just cause or reason that appears

themes: the LORD hath testified against me

schools.summary: Gill: 'so Naomi returned' is the narrator's own summary to excite attention to what follows, not a second return as Aben Ezra supposed.
Clarke dates the arrival just after Passover, prizes the Targum's wave-sheaf gloss, and argues the story's artless simplicity proves its genuineness.
Ellicott: barley, the earliest harvest, about the end of April — the wayfarers arrive to receive their share of the restored blessing.
Henry: they came very seasonably — our times are in God's hand, both the events and the time of them.
